a: God has spoken finally in His Son, who is superior to the angels through whom the law came and greater than Moses as a son over God's house rather than a servant in it. Woven through the argument are urgent warnings not to neglect so great a salvation nor harden the heart in unbelief as the wilderness generation did, but to strive to enter God's promised rest. The section closes by pointing to Jesus, our great high priest who sympathizes with our weakness, inviting us to draw near to the throne of grace.
summary: Christ is superior to angels and to Moses, with warnings not to drift but to enter God's rest, leading to Jesus our high priest.

a: Jesus is appointed high priest after the order of Melchizedek, not Aaron, learning obedience through suffering and becoming the source of eternal salvation. After rebuking spiritual immaturity and warning against falling away, the writer urges believers to hold fast the sure hope anchored in God's oath. He then expounds Melchizedek as a type whose priesthood, without genealogy or end, surpasses the Levitical order and points to Christ's permanent, perfect priesthood.
summary: Jesus is the eternal high priest after the order of Melchizedek, superior to the Levitical priesthood.

a: Christ ministers in the true heavenly sanctuary as mediator of a better covenant founded on better promises, the new covenant that makes the first obsolete. The old tabernacle and its repeated animal sacrifices could not truly cleanse the conscience, but Christ entered the holy place once for all by His own blood, securing eternal redemption. Because His single offering has perfected those being sanctified, the writer exhorts believers to draw near in full assurance, hold fast their confession, and not shrink back.
summary: Christ mediates a better covenant and offers His own blood once for all, perfecting believers and securing eternal redemption.

a: The great roll call of faith portrays Old Testament saints who lived by trust in God's unseen promises, commending faith as the assurance of things hoped for. Surrounded by this cloud of witnesses, believers are called to run with endurance, fixing their eyes on Jesus and accepting the Father's discipline. The letter ends with practical exhortations to brotherly love, hospitality, purity, contentment, and steady worship through Christ, our unchanging shepherd, outside the camp.
summary: Faith's heroes inspire endurance fixed on Jesus, closing with discipline and practical exhortations to love and worship.

a: James opens by reframing trials as occasions for joy that produce steadfastness, urging believers to ask God for wisdom and to be doers of the word, not hearers only. True religion cares for orphans and widows and keeps oneself unstained by the world. He then condemns favoritism toward the rich as a violation of the royal law of love and argues that genuine faith inevitably shows itself in works, since faith without works is dead.
summary: Trials yield steadfastness and believers must be doers of the word, shunning favoritism, for faith without works is dead.

a: James warns about the tongue, a small but destructive fire that no one can tame, and contrasts earthly, self-seeking wisdom with the gentle, peace-loving wisdom from above. He traces quarrels to selfish desires and friendship with the world, calling for humble submission to God and repentance. The letter closes by rebuking oppressive rich employers, urging patience until the Lord's coming, and commending prayer for the sick and confession among believers.
summary: James addresses the untamed tongue, heavenly wisdom, worldliness, and calls for patience and prayer awaiting the Lord.

a: Peter blesses God for the living hope and imperishable inheritance secured by Christ's resurrection, a hope that sustains believers through trials that test and refine their faith. Redeemed by the precious blood of Christ, they are called to be holy, to love one another earnestly, and to crave the pure milk of the word. He describes them as living stones built on Christ the cornerstone, a chosen people and royal priesthood called to honorable conduct, even submitting to authorities and following Christ's example in suffering.
summary: Believers have a living hope and inheritance, called to holiness and honorable conduct as a chosen people built on Christ.

a: Peter instructs wives and husbands, then calls all believers to gentle, reverent witness, always ready to give a reason for their hope and willing to suffer for doing good as Christ did. He urges holy living in light of the coming end, fervent love, and faithful use of gifts, viewing fiery trials as sharing in Christ's sufferings. The letter closes by exhorting elders to shepherd God's flock willingly, the young to humility, and all to cast their anxieties on the God who cares for them, resisting the devil and standing firm in grace.
summary: Believers witness gently and endure suffering as Christ did, while elders shepherd the flock and all stand firm in grace.

a: John testifies to the Word of life that he has seen and heard, calling believers to walk in the light and confess sin, trusting Christ our advocate and the propitiation for our sins. Knowing God is shown by keeping His commands, especially love for the brethren, and by not loving the world. He marvels that we are called children of God now, urging believers to abide in Christ, practice righteousness, and love one another in deed and truth rather than in word only.
summary: Believers walk in the light, confess sin trusting Christ, and prove they are God's children by obedience and love.

a: John urges testing the spirits and confessing that Jesus Christ has come in the flesh, then dwells on the truth that God is love, shown in sending His Son as the propitiation, so that those who abide in love abide in God. Love for God and love for the brethren are inseparable, and perfect love casts out fear. He closes assuring believers that faith in the Son of God overcomes the world and that they may know they have eternal life.
summary: God is love shown in sending His Son, so believers love one another and may know they have eternal life through faith.

a: John receives a vision of the glorified, exalted Christ walking among seven golden lampstands, holding the seven stars and declaring Himself the living one who holds the keys of death and Hades. Christ then dictates personal letters to seven churches in Asia, commending their faithfulness, rebuking compromise and lukewarmness, and calling each to repent and overcome. Each message ends with a promise to the one who conquers, grounding the whole book's exhortation to perseverance.
summary: The glorified Christ appears among the lampstands and addresses seven churches with commendation, rebuke, and calls to overcome.

a: John is caught up to the heavenly throne room, where God is worshiped by living creatures and elders as Creator and Lord. A sealed scroll can be opened only by the slain yet living Lamb, who is worshiped as worthy to receive power and praise. As the Lamb opens six of the seven seals, conquest, war, famine, death, the cry of martyrs, and cosmic upheaval unfold, with an interlude sealing God's servants and revealing a great multitude from every nation standing before the throne.
summary: Heaven's throne room worships God and the slain Lamb, who opens the seals while a redeemed multitude is sealed and saved.

a: The seventh seal gives way to seven trumpets announcing escalating judgments on the earth, sea, waters, heavens, and humanity, including plagues of locusts and demonic hordes that still do not bring repentance. An interlude features a mighty angel with a little scroll that John eats, and the measuring of God's temple, along with two witnesses who prophesy, are killed, and are raised. The seventh trumpet sounds, announcing that the kingdom of the world has become the kingdom of our Lord and of His Christ.
summary: Seven trumpets unleash escalating judgments, framing two witnesses and the announcement of God's coming reign.

a: A symbolic drama unveils the dragon's war against the woman and her child, depicting Satan's hostility toward Christ and His people, who overcome by the blood of the Lamb. Two beasts arise from sea and earth, demanding worship and marking their followers, while the Lamb stands with the redeemed and angels announce judgment on Babylon. The section culminates in the harvest of the earth and seven bowls of God's wrath poured out in final, intensified plagues.
summary: The dragon and two beasts war against God's people, met by the Lamb's redeemed and the seven bowls of God's wrath.

a: Babylon the great, a harlot riding the beast, symbolizes the seductive, idolatrous world-system whose sudden fall is mourned by kings and merchants but celebrated in heaven. Christ then appears as the rider on the white horse, the King of kings, who defeats the beast and false prophet. The vision continues with Satan's binding and a thousand-year reign, his final release and defeat, and the great white throne judgment where the dead are judged according to their works.
summary: Babylon falls and Christ returns to conquer His foes, followed by the millennial reign, Satan's defeat, and final judgment.

a: John sees a new heaven and a new earth and the holy city, the new Jerusalem, coming down from God, where He dwells with His people and wipes away every tear, ending death, mourning, and pain. The city is described in radiant glory, needing no temple or sun, for God and the Lamb are its light, and the river and tree of life bring healing to the nations. The book closes with the promise that Christ is coming soon and the church's answering cry, come, Lord Jesus.
summary: God makes all things new in the new Jerusalem where He dwells with His people, ending with the promise that Christ comes soon.
